evidence: Action identifiers are duplicated across modules (`actions.Action*` constants in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and string literals in `contracts.ActionContractBindings` in `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go`), creating parallel boundary definitions.

`buildUploadActionContracts` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` indexes a map by expected action constants without presence checks, so registry drift yields zero-value contracts instead of an explicit contract-validation failure.

`contracts.ValidateActionContractBindings()` exists but is not invoked by the action-contract initialization path; `ensureActionContractsInitialized` instead panics on first access if initialization fails.

README quality-gate text marks `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` as governed by `Runtime Contract Gate`, but the documented targeted checks are upload/startram-specific and do not describe C2C contract-compatibility checks despite C2C being in the shared registry.

Function-bag runtimes are heavily fragmented and repeated across modules (for example `Runtime*Ops` families in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` lines 15-187, `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 69-93, and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` lines 346-366), creating high indirection overhead before domain behavior starts.

Several modules add pass-through wrappers that mostly forward to defaults plus nil checks (for example `start...WithContext`/`...ForRuntime` layers in `goseg/system/wifi.go` lines 227-366 and `goseg/importer/importer.go` lines 1016-1200), increasing call depth without adding policy.

Compatibility aggregate models still leak through business code: `structs.UrbitDocker` embeds six sub-configs (`goseg/structs/configs.go` lines 147-154), while callers in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` lines 278-395 and `goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go` lines 127-152 repeatedly unpack/repack slices of it to persist narrowly scoped updates.

goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go contains a long sequence of exported wrappers that mostly validate then forward to runtime methods (e.g., GetContainerImageTag/GetContainerRunningStatus/DeleteContainer/StartContainer/CreateContainer/ExecDockerCommand at lines 83-208).

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go centralizes many unrelated seams (container lifecycle, urbit config, snapshot, file, image, volume, timers) in one registry and default builder (lines 30-254), which increases structural coupling.

goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go mixes error-returning validation with panic-based accessors and constructors (ensureConfigured returns error at lines 138-157, while ConstructAppsInfo/ConstructProfileInfo/ConstructSystemInfo panic at lines 113-136, and startramSettings/startramConfig panic at lines 249-260).

System package files blend state management, OS command execution, and long-running loop orchestration in the same modules (goseg/system/wifi.go lines 17-337, goseg/system/system.go lines 52-253, goseg/system/disk.go lines 22-270).

findings.summary: Action identity is modeled in both `actions` and `contracts`, creating boundary drift risk between protocol tokens and contract bindings.
evidence: `actions.go` declares action tokens and namespaces as local constants.
`contracts.go` independently declares action namespaces plus `ActionContractBindings` with string action IDs.
No compile-time or validation path enforces that `actions.Action*` constants remain exactly aligned with `contracts.ActionContractBindings` entries.
findings.suggestion: Promote `contracts.ActionContractBindings` (or a generated schema) to the single source of truth and derive exported action enums/views from it, eliminating duplicated token declarations across packages.

findings.summary: Upload action policy wiring is hardcoded in `actions` and not structurally tied to the contract binding catalog.
evidence: `buildUploadActionContracts` constructs a fixed two-item slice keyed to `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint` and `ActionUploadReset`.

The contract side already has a generic namespace binding registry (`ActionContractBindingsForNamespace`) but payload policy is not encoded there, forcing out-of-band updates when upload actions change.
Missing keys in `byAction` default to zero-value `ActionContract`, so drift can degrade behavior silently instead of failing at boundary definition time.
findings.suggestion: Move upload payload requirements into the shared contract/binding model (or a dedicated schema table in `contracts`) and generate `UploadActionContract` entries from that shared metadata with explicit validation for missing bindings.

findings.summary: Global lazy initialization with panic-based failure couples all action metadata consumers to a process-wide fatal path.
evidence: `ensureActionContractsInitialized` uses package globals plus `sync.Once` and calls `panic` if initialization fails.

Initialization depends on `contracts.ActionContractBindingsForNamespace` and `contracts.ActionContractDescriptor`, so defects in contract wiring propagate as runtime panics in unrelated callers that only read action metadata.
This creates hidden cross-module control-flow coupling instead of explicit error-bearing construction boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Replace panic-on-first-use global initialization with explicit constructor/bootstrap validation that returns errors, then inject the validated registry into consumers.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency seams are over-fragmented into large function-bag structs, adding indirection cost without proportional behavioral isolation.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many narrowly sliced `Runtime*Ops` structs with dozens of function fields (lines 15-187) and then composes defaults mostly as direct function references (lines 256-384).
`importerRuntime` has 20+ callback fields and a long `ensureForWorkflow` gate (lines 69-202) before routing into mostly forwarded operations.
`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` also carries a large callback surface plus repetitive `withDefaults` assignment chain (lines 346-427), duplicating the same seam pattern seen in other modules.
findings.suggestion: Collapse each subsystem to a smaller capability interface (coarser-grained collaborators) and centralize default wiring in one constructor per bounded context; keep fine-grained seams only where tests need explicit substitution.

findings.summary: The compatibility aggregate `UrbitDocker` leaks domain boundaries, forcing repeated manual projection logic for targeted updates.
evidence: `UrbitDocker` embeds runtime/network/schedule/web/feature/backup configs into one aggregate type (lines 147-154 in `structs/configs.go`).
`operations_runtime.go` duplicates near-identical persist helpers for each embedded config by copying out a sub-config, mutating it, and copying back (lines 278-395).
`rectify_services.go` similarly mirrors manual persistence projection when reconciling web/network fields (lines 127-152), showing callers must understand internal aggregate shape.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit repository/update methods per concern (runtime/network/web/feature/schedule/backup) and migrate call sites away from aggregate mutation fallbacks; keep `UrbitDocker` as serialization compatibility only.

findings.summary: Runtime abstraction contracts are inconsistent (panic, zero-value fallback, and error-return styles coexist), making seams dishonest for callers.
evidence: `importerRuntime` methods panic for missing callbacks in several accessors (`swapSettings`, `startramSettings`, `fileStat`, etc., lines 204-315), despite also providing `ensureForWorkflow` error checks.
`collectorRuntime` accessor methods also panic on missing runtime callbacks (for example `startramSettings`, `startramConfig`, `penpaiSettings`, lines 249-296).

`RectifyRuntime.startramSettings()` instead silently returns a zero-value settings object when callback is nil (lines 92-97 in `rectify.go`), creating different failure semantics for similar runtime seams.
findings.suggestion: Standardize runtime seam contracts to explicit error returns (no panics or silent zero defaults) and enforce a single validation path at construction time.

findings.summary: Action contract boundary is split across duplicated literals in actions and contracts packages.
evidence: `actions.go` defines `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `ActionUploadReset`, and `ActionC2CConnect` constants while `contracts.go` separately defines `ActionContractBindings` with raw string actions (`open-endpoint`, `reset`, `connect`).
No compile-time linkage enforces that action constants and binding strings remain synchronized.
findings.suggestion: Make `contracts.ActionContractBindings` the only source of action tokens and generate/derive action enums and namespace-specific accessors from it (or invert ownership so `contracts` imports strongly typed action constants), eliminating duplicated string definitions.

findings.summary: Upload contract assembly silently accepts missing bindings via zero-value map lookups.
evidence: `buildUploadActionContracts` builds `byAction` and then reads `byAction[ActionUploadOpenEndpoint]` and `byAction[ActionUploadReset]` without checking key existence.
If `ActionContractBindings` drifts (rename/remove action), `UploadActionContract` entries can contain empty `ActionContract` data rather than failing contract initialization explicitly.
findings.suggestion: Change `buildUploadActionContracts` to return `([]UploadActionContract, error)` and validate required actions exist before construction; fail initialization with explicit errors tied to missing namespace/action bindings.

findings.summary: Contract registry validation is defined but not enforced in the runtime initialization path.
evidence: `contracts.ValidateActionContractBindings()` performs structural checks (missing descriptors, duplicates) but is never called by `initializeActionContracts`.
`ensureActionContractsInitialized` panics on init error, so invalid contract state is discovered lazily at first use instead of at a deterministic startup/validation boundary.
README describes governance and CI policy around contract surfaces, but runtime code does not enforce this validation contract directly.
findings.suggestion: Call `ValidateActionContractBindings()` as part of action-contract initialization (or startup boot checks) and return typed validation errors instead of panic-based discovery, so governance contracts are enforced before serving traffic.

findings.summary: Documented quality-gate scope emphasizes upload/startram while shared contract registry also governs C2C.
evidence: README backend gate references targeted checks for upload branch matrix, upload dispatch parity, and startram masked-error semantics.

Both `contracts.go` and `actions.go` include C2C action contracts (`ActionNamespaceC2C`, `C2CConnectAction`, `C2CActionContract*` APIs), but no C2C-specific compatibility check is documented in the same governance section.
findings.suggestion: Either narrow the documented contract-governance claim to explicitly exclude C2C, or extend the contract gate/tests to include C2C action contract compatibility checks so docs and enforced boundary policy match.

findings.summary: Wrapper-heavy runtime APIs duplicate forwarding and validation logic across orchestration/auth layers instead of enforcing a single coherent boundary.
evidence: orchestration.go repeats public wrapper + runtime method pairs for many operations (e.g., lines 83-208), creating a broad pass-through surface with duplicated checks.

runtime_ops.go defines a very wide seam graph (container, urbit, snapshot, file, image, command, timer, wireguard, netdata, minio) in one place (lines 30-254), so adding/changing one operation touches central wiring.
auth.go similarly exports many thin pass-through functions into subpackages (e.g., Start/Stop, token/session helpers), spreading policy across wrappers instead of one explicit service interface.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a smaller orchestration/auth service boundary: keep one validated entrypoint per capability group, collapse thin wrappers into internal methods, and move repetitive validation into shared decorators to reduce duplicated call paths.

findings.summary: Collector runtime uses conflicting failure semantics (panic and error return) for the same dependency-missing condition.
evidence: collectors.ensureConfigured returns errors (lines 138-157), but ConstructAppsInfo/ConstructProfileInfo/ConstructSystemInfo panic on that same condition (lines 113-136).

collectorRuntime accessors panic when callbacks are nil (startramSettings/startramConfig/penpaiSettings/swapSettings/backupRoot/backupTime at lines 249-295), while other paths return typed errors (startramServices at lines 263-267).
Nearby config/version flow consistently uses explicit error returns for fetch/resolve/publish paths rather than panic-driven control flow.
findings.suggestion: Standardize collectors on explicit error propagation: remove panic accessors, return errors from all constructors/collectors, and keep one failure contract across runtime validation and data collection paths.

findings.summary: System modules combine stateful orchestration, shell interaction, and domain logic in the same files, producing mixed responsibilities.
evidence: wifi.go holds global mutable state (WifiInfo/Device/c2cEnabled), runtime seam wiring, command execution helpers, and background loop lifecycle in one module (lines 17-337).
system.go combines hardware telemetry, filesystem label parsing, cron management, and shell-command execution utilities in one file (lines 52-253).
disk.go mixes persistent state cache, filesystem formatting/mount orchestration, fstab reconciliation, and direct command seams in one module (lines 22-270).
findings.suggestion: Split system concerns into separate components (state store, command adapters, and orchestration services) and inject command adapters into orchestration functions so process execution and domain workflows are not interleaved.

findings.summary: Logger sink configuration duplicates behavior through parallel APIs, increasing maintenance drift risk.
evidence: logger.go defines both ConfigureLogstreamRuntime (lines 125-133) and configureSystemLogSink (lines 205-213) with effectively the same nil/default assignment logic for sysLogSink.

InitializeWithRuntime already carries substantial startup responsibilities (path resolution, logger replacement, banner, fallback handling; lines 224-270), so duplicated sink entrypoints further fragment lifecycle behavior.
Tests rely on runtime-level behavior; keeping duplicated configuration paths invites divergent test/setup patterns as logger lifecycle evolves.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate sink assignment into a single function used by both public API and internal initialization, then remove the duplicate path to keep logger lifecycle behavior in one place.
